paper_title: Diatom Culture, Observation, and Movement Analysis

methods.description: Procedure for obtaining, subculturing, and maintaining specific diatom species (Nitzschia sp., P. tricornutum, Navicula sp., O. aurita) under controlled laboratory conditions.

steps.description: For the isolated Nitzschia sp. (NIT), we used cells collected in Chiba Prefecture, Japan for the experiment. For the isolated P. tricornutum (PHE), we used cells provided by Laboratory Hanjia at Taiwan Ocean University. For the isolated Navicula sp. (NAV), we used cells collected in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. For the isolated O. aurita (ODO), we used cells purchased from NIES collection, National institute for Environmental Studies (Ibaraki, Japan).
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain specific diatom species from designated sources
inputs: Collection locations/sources (Chiba, Taiwan Ocean University, Shizuoka, NIES collection)
outputs: Initial cultures of Nitzschia sp. (NIT)
Initial cultures of P. tricornutum (PHE)
Initial cultures of Navicula sp. (NAV)
Initial cultures of O. aurita (ODO)

steps.description: To subculture these cells, Guildard's ( f/2) marine water enrichment solution (G9903; Sigma-Aldrich, Munich, Germany) was used. It was dissolved in 1,000 mL of pure water.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare f/2 marine water enrichment solution for culturing
outputs: f/2 culture medium

steps.description: The culture temperature was 21°C, and the culture was irradiated with a white fluorescent lamp of 85 μmol/m 2 /s 1 for 12 h every day.
steps.searchable_summary: Set and maintain diatom culture environmental conditions (temperature, light intensity, photoperiod)
outputs: Controlled culture environment (21°C, 85 μmol/m²/s¹, 12h light/12h dark)

steps.description: For each subculture, 1 mL of diatom cells suspension was transferred to 10 mL of fresh culture solution.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform subculturing by transferring a small volume of cell suspension to fresh medium
outputs: New subculture

steps.description: For the subculture, cycles were every 3 weeks for Nitzschia sp. and Navicula sp., every 1 week for P. tricornutum, every 4 weeks for O. aurita.
steps.searchable_summary: Maintain specific subculture cycles for different diatom species
outputs: Maintained diatom cultures according to schedule

steps.description: Sizes and shapes of the diatom cells were observed by a holographic microscope (HT-2, Tomocube Inc., Deajeon, Korea).
steps.searchable_summary: Observe and record diatom cell size and shape using holographic microscopy
outputs: Diatom cell size and shape data

methods.description: Description of the modified inverted microscope system used for observing diatom cells in a vertical orientation.

steps.description: This "tumbled" optical microscope is a commercial inverted microscope (CKX53; Olympus Co., Tokyo, Japan) tumbled by a homemade stand, and the vertical sample stage of the microscope is changed horizontally Shoumura et al., 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: Modify a commercial inverted microscope using a homemade stand to orient it vertically and change the sample stage orientation
outputs: Modified "tumbled" microscope with horizontal sample stage

steps.description: Microscopic examination of the diatom suspension was performed approximately 10 days after the latest subculture.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform microscopic examination of diatom suspension using the modified microscope at a specific time point after subculture
outputs: Microscopic view of diatom cells

methods.description: Procedure for fabricating a 1 mm³ microchamber used for observing diatom cell movement.

steps.description: A 1 mm 3 microchamber was created by irradiation of the photosensitive chamber (FORTURN, Mainz, Germany) to a laser followed by wet etching (Hanada et al., 2008(Hanada et al., , 2011.
steps.searchable_summary: Fabricate a 1 mm³ microchamber using laser irradiation of photosensitive material and wet etching
outputs: Fabricated 1 mm³ microchamber

steps.description: The fabricated microchamber was glued with a piece of Scotch tape (SWP-15; 3M, St. Paul, MN, USA) inside of the cover of a disposable Petri dish (52w, 12 mm in thickness).
steps.searchable_summary: Glue the fabricated microchamber inside a Petri dish cover
outputs: Petri dish cover with attached microchamber

steps.description: The cover of another Petri dish was arranged with the upper part comprising the microchamber, and the bonding surface was hermetically sealed with parafilm.
steps.searchable_summary: Assemble a second Petri dish cover with the first and hermetically seal the joint
inputs: Petri dish cover with attached microchamber
outputs: Composited and sealed Petri dish structure with microchamber

steps.description: A quarter of the area of the composited Petri dishes was extracted to make a hole to inject a cell suspension.
steps.searchable_summary: Create an injection hole in the composited Petri dish structure
inputs: Composited and sealed Petri dish structure
outputs: Composited Petri dish structure with injection port

methods.description: Procedure for preparing and setting up the microchamber with diluted diatom cell suspension for observation under the "tumbled" microscope.

steps.description: The composited Petri dish was attached with a rubber band to the vertical sample stage of an "tumbled" microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: Attach the microchamber assembly to the microscope sample stage
inputs: Composited Petri dish structure with microchamber
outputs: Microchamber assembly mounted on microscope stage

steps.description: The cell suspension was diluted 40-fold with medium, and 10 mL of the diluted cell suspension was injected into the composited Petri dish.
steps.searchable_summary: Dilute cell suspension and inject into the microchamber
inputs: Composited Petri dish with injection port
outputs: Diluted cell suspension
Microchamber filled with diluted cell suspension (10 mL)

steps.description: Observations of diatom cells were initiated at room temperature using a 20× objective lens under 85 μmol/m 2 /s 1 illumination and within 5 min of injection.
steps.searchable_summary: Initiate microscopic observation under specified conditions (temperature, objective, illumination, timing)
outputs: Live microscopic view of cells under observation conditions

methods.description: Procedure for recording observation movies of floating diatom cells using a video camera.

steps.description: Observation movies of floating diatom cells were recorded for 1 h using a HDR-CX590 video camera (SONY, Tokyo, Japan) at a shutter speed of 1/30 s.
steps.searchable_summary: Record video of cell movement for a specified duration using a specific camera and shutter speed
outputs: Raw video files of cell movement

steps.description: The movies were recorded as AVCHD files.
steps.searchable_summary: Save recorded video files in AVCHD format
outputs: AVCHD video files

methods.description: Procedure for processing recorded observation movies and analyzing diatom cell movement using 2D tracking software, including automatic centroid tracking and manual two-point tracking.

steps.description: The resulting observation movies were analyzed using 2D tracking analysis software (Move-tr/2D, Library, Tokyo, Japan) (Umemura et al., 2013(Umemura et al., , 2015.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze observation movies using 2D tracking software
outputs: Analysis results from tracking software

steps.description: The movies were converted into WMV format, the image contrast was adapted using a background subtraction function, and calibration was made using an image of a scale.
steps.searchable_summary: Convert video format, apply background subtraction for contrast enhancement, and perform spatial calibration
outputs: WMV video files
Contrast-enhanced video
Calibration parameters/settings

steps.description: The first image analysis method was automatic analysis using the centroid mode. The centroid of each cell was automatically tracked every 0.03 s using software, and each centroid was acquired as a function of time (x(t), y(t)) in a two-dimensional rectangular coordinate system.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform automatic cell tracking by detecting and recording centroid coordinates over time
outputs: Time series data of cell centroid coordinates (x(t), y(t))

steps.description: To analyze the movement of diatom cells, 100 individuals were randomly selected for each type of diatom, and a total of the movement of 400 individuals were analyzed using four species of diatoms.
steps.searchable_summary: Select a random subset of tracked individuals for analysis
inputs: Tracking data for all detected individuals
outputs: Tracking data for 100 randomly selected individuals per species (total 400)

steps.description: The second image analysis method was a manual analysis performed to evaluate the rotation of each cell. Both ends of the cell were marked manually (major axis direction) every second, and the coordinates of the two points were recorded as a function of time (x 1 (t), y 1 (t)) and (x 2 (t), y 2 (t)).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform manual tracking of two points on each cell (representing major axis) over time to analyze rotation
outputs: Time series data of two-point coordinates ((x1(t), y1(t)), (x2(t), y2(t)))

steps.description: In the manual analysis, 10 individuals were randomly selected for each type of diatom, and the movement of a total of 40 individuals was analyzed using the four species of diatoms.
steps.searchable_summary: Select a random subset of individuals for manual tracking analysis
outputs: 10 randomly selected individuals per species (total 40) for manual tracking

steps.description: In this two-point analysis, some characteristics of the motionuch as rotation and unrotation-were selected for the analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze motion characteristics, specifically rotation state, from two-point tracking data
inputs: Time series data of two-point coordinates
outputs: Analysis of rotation characteristics (e.g., rotation state over time)

methods.description: Simulation of diatom cell sinking speeds using the traditional Stokes' law and a modified Stokes' law that accounts for differential density of frustule and cytoplasm.

steps.description: The sinking speeds of diatom cells were simulated using the traditional Stokes' law [equation (1)] and the modified Stokes' law [(equation (2)] (Miklasz & Denny, 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Simulate sinking speeds using traditional and modified Stokes' law equations
inputs: Parameters for Stokes' law (Dp, ρtot, ρw, g, η)
Parameters for modified Stokes' law (r, ρcyt, ρft, tv, tg, hv, hg, ρw, g, η)
outputs: Simulated sinking speed values (Stokes)
Simulated sinking speed values (Modified Stokes)

steps.description: The variables used were values based on microscopic images (see Supplementary Fig. 1 and Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain parameter values for simulation from microscopic image measurements and supplementary data
outputs: Parameter values (Dp, r, hv, hg, etc.)

steps.description: Miklasz et al. focused on the difference in mass between diatom frustule and cytoplasm. It is assumed that the thickness of the frustule is constant regardless of the size of the diatom, the mass of the frustule is proportional to the surface area of the cell, and the mass of the cytoplasm is proportional to the volume. Therefore, the density of the entire cell is dominated by the density of the cytoplasm rather than the density of frustule, as the cell size increases. To reflect this fact in the calculations, Miklasz et al. proposed a modified Stokes' law that separated the overall cell density into diatom frustule and cytoplasm.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply the modified Stokes' law model which accounts for differential density contributions of frustule and cytoplasm based on cell size
outputs: Sinking speed calculation incorporating density model
